# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define Numerical Control (NC). List the five basic components of an NC system.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Numerical Control (NC)** is a form of programmable automation in which the processing machine is controlled by numbers, letters, and symbols (coded instructions) that are processed automatically by a Machine Control Unit (MCU). It eliminates the need for manual operation by a skilled human.

The **five basic components** of an NC system are:
1. **Part Program** — The set of coded instructions.
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — The controller that processes the program.
3. **Machine Tool** — The physical cutting machine.
4. **Driving System** — Servo/stepper motors that move the slides.
5. **Feedback System** — Sensors that report actual position (in closed-loop).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop NC systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | **Absent** — MCU assumes position | **Present** — Encoder reports position |
| Motor Type | Stepper motor | Servo motor (DC/AC) |
| Accuracy | Lower (~0.01 mm) | Higher (~0.001 mm or 1 μm) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Error Correction | None | Automatic (PID controller) |
| Application | Light-duty (PCB, small routers) | Heavy-duty (industrial machining centers) |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice (either OR). Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Below are **Question A and Question B** as alternative options.

---

### 📝 **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different **types of NC systems** based on the motion path. Give **two examples** of applications for each type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functions of each component** of a CNC machine tool. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (a):**

The three types of NC systems classified by motion path are:

**1. Point-to-Point (PTP) NC System:**
- The tool moves from one point to another, but the path **between points is uncontrolled** (it may take any trajectory).
- Cutting happens only **after the tool reaches** the programmed position.
- **Examples:** Drilling holes in a printed circuit board (PCB); Boring operations on a gearbox housing; Spot welding in sheet metal; Gun drilling of deep holes.

**2. Straight-Cut NC System:**
- The tool moves in a **controlled straight line**, but only **one axis moves during cutting** (the second axis moves only during non-cutting positioning).
- Suitable for **rectangular profiles and pockets**.
- **Examples:** Face milling of a flat surface; Milling a rectangular slot; Simple turning of cylindrical shafts on a lathe.

**3. Contouring NC System (Continuous Path):**
- The tool moves along a **fully controlled curved path**, with **two or more axes moving simultaneously** during cutting.
- Requires **interpolation** (linear, circular, helical) by the MCU.
- **Examples:** Machining a turbine blade profile; Die cavity machining; 3D sculptured surfaces on a mold; Aerospace structural component milling.

> **Tabular Summary:**

| Type | Axes Moving Simultaneously | Interpolation | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point-to-Point | 0 (one at a time) | Not needed | PCB drilling |
| Straight-Cut | 1 axis during cut | Simple | Pocket milling |
| Contouring | 2+ axes during cut | Linear / Circular | Turbine blade |

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (b):**

The block diagram of a CNC machine tool has **five main components**:

1. **Part Program Input Device:** Modern CNC machines accept programs via **USB, Ethernet, or DNC network**. The program is a text file containing G-codes (geometry) and M-codes (miscellaneous functions).

2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU):** Contains a **microprocessor, memory, and interpolator**. It reads each block of the program, decodes it, and generates pulse signals for the servo drives.

3. **Servo Driving System:** Comprises **servo motors, amplifiers, ball screws, and linear guides**. The amplifier receives low-power signals from the MCU and produces high-power current to drive the motor.

4. **Machine Tool:** The actual mechanical system (lathe, mill, machining center) that holds the workpiece and tool, and performs the cutting.

5. **Feedback System:** **Linear scales** (on slides) and **rotary encoders** (on motors) continuously measure the actual position and send it back to the MCU for **error comparison and correction** (closed-loop control).

The MCU **compares** the commanded position with the actual position from the feedback system. If there is a deviation (error), the **PID controller** adjusts the motor input until the error is minimized.

---

### 📝 **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare **NC and CNC** systems in detail. State **any six points of difference**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the term **'Interpolation'** in CNC. Explain **linear and circular interpolation** with suitable diagrams and example code. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (a):**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | NC | CNC |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Controller | Hard-wired logic (relays, vacuum tubes) | Microprocessor with stored software |
| 2 | Program Storage | Punched paper tape | Electronic memory (RAM/SSD) |
| 3 | Program Editing | Not possible (new tape required) | Possible online (edit at console) |
| 4 | Flexibility | Low — change tape for new part | High — change one line of code |
| 5 | Diagnostics | Minimal | Advanced (error logs, self-test) |
| 6 | Cost & Maintenance | Low initial, high maintenance | High initial, low maintenance |
| 7 | Feedback Loop | Mostly open-loop | Closed-loop with encoders |
| 8 | Networking | Standalone | Networked (Industry 4.0, MTConnect) |

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (b):**

> **Interpolation** is the process of calculating **intermediate tool positions** between the starting point and the ending point of a cut, so that the tool follows a smooth, mathematically defined path.

**1. Linear Interpolation (G01):**
- The tool moves in a **straight line** from the current position to the programmed endpoint.
- Used for **straight cuts, chamfers, tapers**.
- **Example code:**
  ```
  G01 X50.0 Y30.0 F200   ; Move to (50, 30) at 200 mm/min in straight line
  ```
- Parametric form: $x(t) = x_1 + (x_2 - x_1) \cdot t$, $y(t) = y_1 + (y_2 - y_1) \cdot t$

**2. Circular Interpolation (G02/G03):**
- The tool moves along an **arc** of a circle, defined by **endpoint, radius (R), or center (I, J, K)**.
- **G02** = Clockwise (CW) arc.
- **G03** = Counter-clockwise (CCW) arc.
- **Example code:**
  ```
  G02 X60.0 Y40.0 I10.0 J0 F150   ; CW arc to (60,40), center offset (10,0)
  ```
- Used for **rounded corners, fillets, circular pockets, holes**.

---

> [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (and lose 2-3 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing PTP with Straight-Cut:** Students write "in PTP, the path is controlled" — **WRONG**. PTP path is **uncontrolled**; Straight-Cut has a **controlled straight path**.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention feedback** when describing the MCU — the encoder/resolver is **separate** from the MCU, not part of it.
> 3. **Mixing up axis names** — Z is **always parallel to the spindle**, not always vertical. On a lathe, Z is **horizontal**.
> 4. **Not stating units** in derivations (mm vs. μm, m/min vs. mm/min). Examiners **deduct 0.5 mark** for missing units.
> 5. **Forgetting the gear ratio** $i$ in axis resolution derivations — losing 2 marks.
> 6. **Using absolute value symbols `|x|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks table formatting. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE] > **Use this section as your last-minute revision cheat sheet before the exam.**

### 🔑 Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ **NC:** Control of machine tools by coded numerical instructions.
- ✅ **CNC:** NC implemented with a microprocessor and stored program.
- ✅ **MCU:** Machine Control Unit — the "brain" of the NC system.
- ✅ **DNC:** Direct Numerical Control — networked control of multiple CNCs.
- ✅ **Interpolation:** Calculation of intermediate points along a path.
- ✅ **PTP:** Point-to-Point — uncontrolled path between points.
- ✅ **Contouring:** Continuous controlled path with multi-axis motion.
- ✅ **Open-Loop:** No feedback — uses stepper motor.
- ✅ **Closed-Loop:** Has feedback — uses servo + encoder + PID.
- ✅ **Resolution:** Smallest incremental move of an axis (mm/pulse).

### 🔑 Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ $e(t) = x_{cmd} - x_{act}$ — Position error
- ✅ $T_{resolution} = \dfrac{p_{screw}}{N_{step} \cdot i}$ — Axis resolution
- ✅ $F = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2 + F_z^2}$ — Resultant feed rate
- ✅ $v = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ — Cutting speed
- ✅ $f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$ — Feed rate
- ✅ $MRR = f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ — Material Removal Rate

### 🔑 Must-Know Differences
- ✅ **NC vs CNC** (10 points — see Section 5.2)
- ✅ **Open vs Closed Loop** (feedback, motor, accuracy, cost)
- ✅ **PTP vs Straight-Cut vs Contouring** (path control, axes, interpolation)

### 🔑 Must-Know Axis Rules
- ✅ **Z-axis is always parallel to the principal spindle**.
- ✅ **X-axis is the longest travel** (and usually the cutting feed).
- ✅ **Y-axis is perpendicular to both X and Z**.
- ✅ **A, B, C are rotations about X, Y, Z respectively**.
- ✅ **Right-Hand Rule** for determining positive direction.

### 🔑 Must-Remember Numbers
- ✅ First NC machine: **MIT, 1952** (Parsons & Stulen, USAF funded).
- ✅ Modern CNC accuracy: **±1 to ±10 μm**.
- ✅ Standard stepper angle: **1.8°** (200 steps/rev).
- ✅ Common ball screw leads: **5 mm, 10 mm, 20 mm**.
- ✅ Standard NC tape: **1 inch wide, 8-track punched paper tape (EIA RS-244)**.
- ✅ Standard G-code standard: **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267**.

### 🔑 Common Exam Question Patterns
- 📝 "List the five components of an NC system" → **3 marks** (definition + list).
- 📝 "Differentiate NC and CNC" → **7 marks** (table with 6-8 points).
- 📝 "Explain types of NC systems" → **7 marks** (PTP, Straight, Contouring + examples).
- 📝 "Derive axis resolution formula" → **7 marks** (with stepper + ball screw + gear ratio).
- 📝 "Draw block diagram of CNC and explain" → **7 marks** (5 blocks + arrows + feedback loop).
- 📝 "Define interpolation, explain linear and circular" → **7 marks**.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Machine Axis and Coordinate System

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
A **CNC Machine Coordinate System** is a standardized Cartesian reference frame (X, Y, Z) defined on every CNC machine tool to unambiguously specify the position of the cutting tool relative to the workpiece. The system follows the **ISO 841** and **EIA RS-267** standards, which establish a right-handed, three-axis, orthogonal coordinate system plus rotational axes (A, B, C) for tool/workpiece orientation.

The three principal linear axes are defined as:
- **X-axis**: Longest traverse (primary motion along the worktable)
- **Y-axis**: Secondary linear motion, perpendicular to X
- **Z-axis**: Parallel to the principal spindle axis (always aligned with the spindle rotation)

The three principal rotational axes are:
- **A-axis**: Rotation about the X-axis
- **B-axis**: Rotation about the Y-axis
- **C-axis**: Rotation about the Z-axis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** The Z-axis is ALWAYS defined as the axis of rotation of the main spindle. This is non-negotiable in KTU examinations and is the most frequently asked 2-mark question.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a room. The floor has a tile pattern — moving **left-right** is the X-axis, moving **forward-backward** is the Y-axis, and moving **up-down** (ceiling/floor) is the Z-axis. Now, hold a pen in your hand and rotate it: that spinning is rotation about the Z-axis (called C-axis rotation). If you tilt your wrist side-to-side, that's A-axis rotation; if you tilt up-and-down like a nodding head, that's B-axis rotation. CNC machines work exactly the same way — every motion is described in this 6-dimensional space (3 linear + 3 rotational).

## 1.2 Right-Hand Rule Convention

The **Right-Hand Rule (RHR)** is the fundamental convention for defining positive axis directions in CNC. To apply it:
- **Thumb** points along the **+X axis**
- **Index finger** points along the **+Y axis**  
- **Middle finger** points along the **+Z axis**

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** "XYZ = TIM" — **T**humb = X, **I**ndex = Y, **M**iddle = Z. This appears in nearly every KTU Module 3 question paper.

## 1.3 Degrees of Freedom in CNC Machines

| Machine Type | Linear Axes | Rotational Axes | Total DOF | Typical Application |
|--------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 2-Axis Lathe (Turning) | X, Z | — | 2 | Shafts, cylindrical parts |
| 3-Axis Milling | X, Y, Z | — | 3 | Prismatic parts, pockets |
| 4-Axis Mill | X, Y, Z | A or B | 4 | Helical surfaces, impellers |
| 5-Axis Mill | X, Y, Z | A + C or B + C | 5 | Turbine blades, aerospace |
| Turning Center | X, Z | C | 3 | Live tooling, milling-turning |

## 1.4 CNC — Formal Definition

**Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** is the automated control of machine tools by means of a computer executing a stored program of numerical commands. The numerical data represents positions, feed rates, spindle speeds, and tool changes required to manufacture a specific part.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a CNC machine as a **robotic chef** following a **digital recipe card**. The recipe (G-code) tells the chef (machine) which knife (tool) to use, how fast to chop (spindle speed), and exactly where to cut (X, Y, Z coordinates). Unlike a human, the CNC chef can repeat the same recipe 10,000 times with micrometer accuracy, 24/7, without getting tired or distracted.

## 1.5 Classification of CNC Machine Tools

### 1.5.1 Based on Motion Type

**Point-to-Point (PTP) / Positioning Systems:**
- Tool moves to a defined location, then performs the operation
- No controlled path during transit
- Example: Drilling machine, tapping

**Straight-Cut Control:**
- Tool can move in straight lines along axis-aligned directions
- Limited to producing rectangular features
- Example: Simple milling

**Contouring / Continuous Path Control:**
- Tool follows a continuous 3D toolpath
- Multiple axes move simultaneously with interpolation
- Example: Complex die/mold machining

### 1.5.2 Based on Loop Control
- **Open Loop**: Stepper motors with no feedback (cheaper, less accurate)
- **Closed Loop**: Servo motors with position feedback (high accuracy)

## 1.6 Principle of Operation of CNC

The fundamental operating sequence of a CNC machine is:
1. **Input**: Part program (G-code) loaded via USB, network, or DNC
2. **Parsing**: Controller decodes the program into machine instructions
3. **Interpolation**: Trajectory planner generates smooth path points between start and end coordinates
4. **Drive Actuation**: Servo/stepper motors receive electrical pulses to move axes
5. **Feedback**: Encoders/scale systems report actual position
6. **Comparison**: Error signal computed (commanded vs. actual)
7. **Correction**: Position error drives motor until zero
8. **Cutting**: Tool removes material along the verified path

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2-Mark Standard Question:** "List any four basic elements of a CNC system." Answer: (1) Part Program, (2) Controller (MCU), (3) Servo Drives, (4) Feedback Devices, (5) Machine Tool, (6) Tool Magazine.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Axis Nomenclature (ISO 841 Standard)

### 2.1.1 For Vertical Spindle Milling Machines (Most Common in KTU)
- **+Z**: Upward (tool moving away from workpiece)
- **+X**: To the right (viewed from front of machine)
- **+Y**: Away from operator (into the machine)

### 2.1.2 For Turning Centers
- **+Z**: Toward tailstock (away from chuck)
- **+X**: Tool moving away from spindle axis (radial)
- **+Y**: Completes right-hand rule

### 2.1.3 For Horizontal Spindle Machines
- **+Z**: Along spindle, away from workpiece
- **+X**: To the right (operator viewpoint)
- **+Y**: Completes right-hand rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** Whether Z is +up or +down depends on whether the machine is a milling machine or a lathe. This is one of the most common sources of confusion in KTU exams. **Milling: +Z is UP. Turning: +Z is toward tailstock.**

## 2.2 Workpiece vs. Tool Reference

There are two ways axes can be programmed:
- **Tool Moves, Workpiece Stationary**: Default for most mills. Coordinates describe tool position.
- **Workpiece Moves, Tool Stationary**: Common in turning centers where the chuck rotates.

**Equivalent Programming Logic:**

$$X_{tool} = X_{workpiece} + \text{offset}$$

where **offset** is the tool length compensation value stored in the controller.

## 2.3 Coordinate Systems Hierarchy

| Level | System | Description | KTU Significance |
|-------|--------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Machine Coordinate System (MCS)** | Fixed origin at machine home position | Set at manufacture; rarely changed |
| 2 | **Workpiece Coordinate System (WCS)** | Origin at a feature on the workpiece | Programmed via G54–G59 |
| 3 | **Local Coordinate System** | User-defined origin within WCS | G52 command |
| 4 | **Tool Coordinate System** | Tip of cutting tool as origin | TLO via G43/H-code |

## 2.4 CNC Machine Tool Construction Features

### 2.4.1 Machine Structure (Beds and Frames)
- **Cast Iron Bed**: High damping, vibration absorption, used in conventional machines
- **Polymer Concrete Bed**: Better thermal stability, 6× better damping than cast iron
- **Granite Bed**: Used in precision grinders, low thermal expansion (~5×10⁻⁶ /°C)

### 2.4.2 Guideways
- **Sliding Guideways**: Friction-based, high damping, lower speed (≤30 m/min)
- **Rolling Element Guideways**: Ball/roller bearings, higher speed (≥60 m/min), less damping
- **Hydrostatic Guideways**: Oil film, no metal-to-metal contact, highest precision

### 2.4.3 Spindles
- Spindle runout tolerance: **< 0.005 mm** for precision CNC
- Maximum speeds: 12,000 RPM (standard), 40,000 RPM (high-speed), 100,000 RPM (HSM)
- Bearing type: Ceramic hybrid ball bearings (low thermal growth)

### 2.4.4 Tool Magazine
- **Carousel Type**: 16–24 tools, simple, slow change (~5 sec)
- **Chain Type**: 40–120 tools, faster change (~1.5 sec)
- **Matrix Type**: 200+ tools, used in FMS

## 2.5 CNC Drives

### 2.5.1 Stepper Motor Drives (Open Loop)
- **Pulse input → Discrete angular step**
- Common step angles: **1.8°** (200 steps/rev) or **0.75°** (480 steps/rev)
- No feedback, no error correction
- Used in hobby machines, low-cost CNC

### 2.5.2 Servo Motor Drives (Closed Loop)
- **DC Servo**: Older, replaced by AC
- **AC Servo**: Industry standard; uses sinusoidal PWM at 8–16 kHz
- **Linear Motor**: Direct drive, no ballscrew, highest precision

### 2.5.3 Ballscrew Drive
Converts rotary motion to linear motion with high efficiency.

**Lead (l)**: Distance traveled per revolution of the screw.

$$v = N \cdot l$$

where $v$ is linear velocity, $N$ is rotational speed (rev/sec), $l$ is lead (mm/rev).

**Position Resolution:**

$$\delta = \frac{l}{N_{steps}}$$

where $N_{steps}$ is the number of encoder pulses per revolution.

## 2.6 CNC Controller Architecture

A **CNC Controller** (also called MCU — Machine Control Unit) consists of:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**: Decodes G-code, performs interpolation
2. **Memory (RAM/ROM)**: Stores part programs, parameters, compensation tables
3. **Operator Panel (HMI)**: Display + keyboard for input/monitoring
4. **Servo Control Module**: Generates drive signals for each axis
5. **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)**: Handles auxiliary functions (coolant, tool change)
6. **Feedback Interface**: Reads encoders, scales, probes

### 2.6.1 Interpolation Algorithms

**Linear Interpolation (G01):** Moves tool in a straight line from current position to target.

$$P(t) = P_0 + t \cdot (P_1 - P_0), \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

**Circular Interpolation (G02/G03):** Moves tool along an arc.

$$P(\theta) = (R\cos\theta, R\sin\theta), \quad \theta \in [\theta_0, \theta_1]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **G02** = Clockwise arc, **G03** = Counter-clockwise arc. KTU exams test this in nearly every paper.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---------|-------------|-----------|-------|
| $v_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot z$ | Feed rate calculation | $v_f$ = feed (mm/min), $N$ = RPM, $f_z$ = feed/tooth, $z$ = no. of teeth | mm/min |
| $N = \frac{v_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | Spindle speed from cutting speed | $v_c$ = cutting speed, $D$ = tool diameter | m/min, mm |
| $\delta = l / N_{steps}$ | Position resolution | $l$ = lead, $N_{steps}$ = pulses/rev | mm |
| $v = N \cdot l$ | Linear velocity from screw | $N$ = rev/sec, $l$ = lead | mm/sec |
| $T = \frac{F_c \cdot r}{9550}$ | Spindle torque (DC motor) | $F_c$ = cutting force, $r$ = radius, $T$ = torque | N·m, N, mm |
| $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ | Material removal rate | $v_f$ = feed, $a_p$ = depth, $a_e$ = width | mm³/min |

## 2.8 Engineering Utility

The CNC coordinate system is the universal language of manufacturing. Every modern CAD/CAM system generates toolpaths using these same conventions. Mastery of axis nomenclature is essential for:
- **Aerospace**: 5-axis machining of turbine blades
- **Automotive**: High-volume production of engine blocks
- **Medical**: Custom implants with sub-50 μm tolerances
- **Tool & Die**: Complex mold cavities with ±5 μm accuracy

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

**Problem:** A 25 mm diameter end mill is to be used on mild steel with a recommended cutting speed of $v_c = 80$ m/min. Calculate the required spindle speed.

**Given:**
- Tool diameter: $D = 25$ mm
- Cutting speed: $v_c = 80$ m/min

**Step 1:** Recall the fundamental relationship between cutting speed and spindle speed.

The cutting speed is the tangential velocity at the tool periphery:

$$v_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

where $N$ is spindle speed in revolutions per minute (RPM).

**Step 2:** Convert units to be consistent. Since $D$ is in mm and $v_c$ is in m/min, multiply $D$ by $10^{-3}$ or convert the formula.

$$v_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$N = \frac{v_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$N = \frac{80 \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot 25}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the denominator.

$$\pi \cdot 25 = 78.5398 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Divide.

$$N = \frac{80000}{78.5398} = 1018.59 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6:** Round to nearest available spindle speed.

$$N \approx 1000 \text{ RPM} \text{ (or 1020 RPM if available)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the unit conversion step. Many students lose 1 mark by writing "$v_c = \pi D N$" without converting mm to m.

## 3.2 Derivation: Feed Rate Calculation

**Problem:** A 4-tooth end mill ($z = 4$) operates at $N = 1000$ RPM with a feed per tooth of $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth. Calculate the table feed rate.

**Step 1:** Identify variables.
- $N = 1000$ RPM
- $z = 4$ teeth
- $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth

**Step 2:** Apply the feed rate formula.

$$v_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot z$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$v_f = 1000 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 4$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$v_f = 200 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to mm/sec if required.

$$v_f = \frac{200}{60} = 3.33 \text{ mm/sec}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Position Resolution of CNC Axis

**Problem:** A ballscrew has a lead of $l = 10$ mm/rev and is driven by a servo motor with an encoder of $N_{steps} = 2500$ pulses/rev. The controller applies 4× electronic gearing. Find the position resolution.

**Step 1:** Identify effective pulses per revolution.

$$N_{effective} = N_{steps} \times \text{quadrature factor}$$

Quadrature encoding multiplies the line count by 4:

$$N_{effective} = 2500 \times 4 = 10000 \text{ pulses/rev}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the resolution formula.

$$\delta = \frac{l}{N_{effective}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$\delta = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{10000} = 0.001 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Express in micrometers.

$$\delta = 1 \text{ μm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This 1 μm resolution is the industry standard for high-precision CNC. Premium machines use linear scales with 0.1 μm resolution.

## 3.4 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Milling

**Problem:** Calculate MRR for a face milling operation with:
- Feed rate: $v_f = 500$ mm/min
- Axial depth of cut: $a_p = 2$ mm
- Radial depth (width): $a_e = 50$ mm

**Step 1:** State the MRR formula.

$$MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$MRR = 500 \cdot 2 \cdot 50$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$MRR = 50000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to cm³/min.

$$MRR = 50 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to SI (m³/sec).

$$MRR = \frac{50000}{10^6 \cdot 60} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/sec}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Drive Comparison

| Drive Type | Control Mode | Accuracy | Cost | Speed | KTU Use Case |
|------------|--------------|----------|------|-------|--------------|
| Stepper Motor | Open Loop | ±0.05 mm | Low | Low | Plotter, 3D printer, hobby CNC |
| AC Servo + Ballscrew | Closed Loop | ±0.001 mm | High | High | Industrial CNC, machine tools |
| Linear Motor | Closed Loop | ±0.0005 mm | Very High | Very High | High-speed machining, precision grinders |
| Hydraulic Servo | Closed Loop | ±0.01 mm | Medium | High | Large machines, presses |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: 2D vs. 3D Machining

| Parameter | 2D Machining (2.5 Axis) | 3D Machining (3-Axis) | 5-Axis Machining |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Axes moving simultaneously | 2 linear (X, Y) + Z indexed | X, Y, Z continuously | All 5 axes interpolated |
| Z-axis movement | Stepped (layer by layer) | Continuous with X, Y | Continuous with A, B, C |
| Tool orientation | Fixed vertical | Fixed vertical | Any angle in 3D space |
| Typical parts | Plate, profile, pocketing | Die cavities, mold inserts | Turbine blades, impellers |
| Programming complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| KTU exam frequency | High | Very High | Medium |

## 3.7 Verification Example: Closed-Loop Position Control

**Step 1:** Commanded position: $X_c = 100.000$ mm
**Step 2:** Feedback position: $X_f = 99.998$ mm
**Step 3:** Position error:

$$e = X_c - X_f = 0.002 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Controller computes correction pulse to motor:

$$\Delta N = \frac{e}{\delta} = \frac{0.002}{0.001} = 2 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 5:** Motor advances 2 pulses, encoder confirms new position, $e \to 0$.

This loop runs thousands of times per second on modern CNC.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define CNC and explain the right-hand rule for axis convention in CNC machine tools.**

**Model Answer:**
CNC stands for **Computer Numerical Control**. It refers to the automation of machine tools through a computer that executes a stored program of numerical commands. The right-hand rule defines positive axis directions:
- **Thumb** → +X axis
- **Index finger** → +Y axis
- **Middle finger** → +Z axis

The Z-axis is always parallel to the main spindle axis. For a vertical milling machine, +Z points upward. For a turning center, +Z points from chuck to tailstock. Rotational axes A, B, C rotate about X, Y, Z respectively.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Q: Differentiate between point-to-point and continuous path CNC control systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Continuous Path (Contouring) |
|---------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Tool path | Discrete points | Smooth continuous path |
| Axis motion | Sequential | Simultaneous (interpolated) |
| Applications | Drilling, boring, tapping | Milling complex contours |
| Accuracy | High position accuracy | High path accuracy |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Example | Drilling machine | 3D mold machining |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the constructional features of a CNC machining center in detail, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of the bed, column, guideways, spindle, and automatic tool changer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the principle of operation of a CNC machine tool. Explain linear and circular interpolation with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **CNC Machining Center** integrates multiple machining operations (milling, drilling, boring, tapping) in a single setup. Its constructional features include:

**1. Bed and Column:**
- Made of **polymer concrete or cast iron** for vibration damping
- The **bed** is the foundation supporting the worktable
- The **column** is the vertical structure supporting the spindle head
- Reinforced with internal ribs for rigidity

**2. Guideways:**
- **Linear motion guideways** with rolling elements (ball/roller)
- Preloaded to eliminate backlash
- Provide repeatability of ±0.005 mm

**3. Spindle Assembly:**
- Direct-drive AC motor (no belt) for high-speed operations
- Speed range: 0 to 12,000 RPM (standard), up to 40,000 RPM (HSM)
- Ceramic hybrid bearings for thermal stability
- Spindle taper: BT-40, BT-50, HSK-A63, or CAT-40

**4. Automatic Tool Changer (ATC):**
- **Carousel type** (16–24 tools, 5–8 sec tool change)
- **Chain type** (40+ tools, 1.5 sec tool change)
- Uses a robotic arm with double grippers
- Tool identification via coded ring or RFID chip

**5. Worktable:**
- Travels in X and Y axes
- Equipped with T-slots for fixture mounting
- Can rotate (4th axis) on machining centers with trunnion

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Principle of Operation:**
A CNC machine operates on the principle of **closed-loop feedback control**. The sequence is:

1. The **part program** (G-code) is input to the controller
2. The **MCU** decodes each block of code
3. The **interpolator** generates intermediate points between start and end positions
4. The **servo drives** receive pulse signals proportional to required motion
5. **Servo motors** rotate the ballscrews, moving the slides
6. **Encoders** report actual position back to the controller
7. The **comparator** computes the error (commanded − actual)
8. The error drives the motor until position error is zero

**Linear Interpolation (G01):**
The tool moves in a straight line from the current point to a programmed endpoint. The interpolator generates intermediate points along the line:

$$P(t) = (1-t) \cdot P_0 + t \cdot P_1, \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

For a 2D move from (0, 0) to (100, 50):
- At $t = 0.5$: $P = (50, 25)$
- At $t = 0.25$: $P = (25, 12.5)$

Feed rate is constant along the path.

**Circular Interpolation (G02/G03):**
The tool moves along an arc. G02 = Clockwise (CW), G03 = Counter-Clockwise (CCW).

For an arc of radius R in XY plane:

$$x(\theta) = R \cos\theta + x_{center}$$
$$y(\theta) = R \sin\theta + y_{center}$$

The interpolator computes $\Delta x = R(\cos\theta_2 - \cos\theta_1)$ and $\Delta y = R(\sin\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1)$ at each sampling instant.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** What are the different types of CNC machine tool drives? Compare stepper motor and servo motor drives. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the G-code and M-code structure used in CNC part programming. Write a sample part program to face turn and step turn a cylindrical workpiece. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Types of CNC Drives:**

**1. Stepper Motor Drive (Open Loop):**
- Operates in open loop — no feedback
- Converts electrical pulses into discrete angular steps
- Standard step angle: **1.8° (200 steps/rev)**
- Holding torque maintains position when stopped
- No error correction → potential missed steps

**2. DC Servo Motor Drive (Closed Loop):**
- Uses DC motor with encoder feedback
- Constant torque over wide speed range
- Older technology, brush wear limits life

**3. AC Servo Motor Drive (Closed Loop):**
- Industry standard for modern CNC
- Uses brushless synchronous AC motor
- Encoder feedback at 17-bit resolution (131,072 pulses/rev)
- Excellent high-speed performance

**4. Linear Motor Drive (Closed Loop):**
- Direct linear motion — no ballscrew
- Highest speed and accuracy
- Used in high-speed machining centers

**5. Hydraulic Servo Drive:**
- Uses hydraulic cylinder + servo valve
- High force capability
- Used in large machines

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| Control | Open loop | Closed loop |
| Feedback | None | Encoder required |
| Accuracy | ±0.05 mm | ±0.001 mm |
| Speed | Lower (≤1000 RPM) | High (≥3000 RPM) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Missed steps | Yes | No (auto-corrected) |
| Holding torque | Decreases with speed | Constant |
| Application | Hobby CNC, plotters | Industrial CNC |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**G-Code and M-Code Structure:**

**G-Codes (Preparatory Functions):** Define the type of motion or operation
- G00: Rapid traverse (non-cutting)
- G01: Linear interpolation (cutting feed)
- G02: Circular interpolation CW
- G03: Circular interpolation CCW
- G90: Absolute programming
- G91: Incremental programming
- G94: Feed per minute mode
- G95: Feed per revolution mode

**M-Codes (Miscellaneous Functions):** Control auxiliary machine functions
- M03: Spindle ON (CW)
- M04: Spindle ON (CCW)
- M05: Spindle OFF
- M06: Tool change
- M08: Coolant ON
- M09: Coolant OFF
- M30: Program end and rewind

**Part Program Block Format:**

```
N10 G00 X_ Z_  (Block number, prep code, coordinates)
```

**Sample Part Program — Facing and Step Turning:**

```
O1001 (PROGRAM NUMBER)
N05 G21 G97 G40 (MM, SPINDLE RPM, TOOL COMP OFF)
N10 G28 U0 W0 (RETURN TO REFERENCE POINT)
N15 T0101 (TOOL 1, OFFSET 1 - FACING TOOL)
N20 M03 S1500 (SPINDLE CW AT 1500 RPM)
N25 G00 X52.0 Z2.0 (RAPID TO START)
N30 G00 X55.0 (RAPID TO FACE START)
N35 G01 X-1.0 F0.15 (FACE TURN AT 0.15 MM/REV)
N40 G00 X52.0 Z2.0 (RETRACT)
N45 T0202 (TOOL 2 - TURNING TOOL)
N50 G00 X20.0 Z2.0 (RAPID TO TURNING START)
N55 G01 Z-25.0 F0.20 (LONGITUDINAL FEED)
N60 G01 X30.0 (RADIAL FEED OUT)
N65 G00 Z2.0 (RETRACT IN Z)
N70 G00 X0 (RAPID TO CENTER)
N75 G01 Z0 F0.10 (FINISHING PASS)
N80 G00 X100.0 Z100.0 (RAPID TO SAFE POSITION)
N85 M05 (SPINDLE OFF)
N90 M09 (COOLANT OFF)
N95 M30 (PROGRAM END)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Missing M30** at end → Lose 1 mark (program must terminate properly)
> - **Wrong spindle direction** (M04 instead of M03 for standard turning) → Lose 0.5 mark
> - **Confusing G90 vs G91** (absolute vs incremental) → Major marks lost; always specify the mode
> - **Not using block numbers (N-codes)** → Lose 0.5 mark
> - **Forgetting G21/G20 (metric/imperial)** → Lose 0.5 mark
> - **Missing tool offset (T0101)** → Controller will not apply tool compensation

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist

**Coordinate System Basics:**
- ☐ Z-axis = Spindle axis (ALWAYS)
- ☐ Right-hand rule: Thumb = X, Index = Y, Middle = Z
- ☐ A rotates about X, B about Y, C about Z
- ☐ G54–G59 = Work coordinate systems
- ☐ G43 = Tool length offset

**Machine Types:**
- ☐ 2-axis: Lathe (X, Z)
- ☐ 3-axis: Mill (X, Y, Z)
- ☐ 4-axis: Mill + rotational (A or B)
- ☐ 5-axis: Mill + 2 rotational axes

**Control Types:**
- ☐ PTP: Drilling, tapping (no path control)
- ☐ Straight-cut: 2D rectangular moves
- ☐ Contouring: Continuous 3D path

**Drive Systems:**
- ☐ Stepper: Open loop, ±0.05 mm
- ☐ Servo: Closed loop, ±0.001 mm
- ☐ Linear motor: Direct drive, highest precision
- ☐ Resolution = Lead / Pulses per rev

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $N = \frac{v_c \cdot 1000}{\pi D}$ — Spindle speed
- ☐ $v_f = N \cdot f_z \cdot z$ — Feed rate
- ☐ $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot a_e$ — Material removal rate
- ☐ $\delta = \frac{l}{N_{steps}}$ — Position resolution

**Part Programming Essentials:**
- ☐ G00 = Rapid (no cutting)
- ☐ G01 = Linear cutting feed
- ☐ G02 = CW arc, G03 = CCW arc
- ☐ M03 = Spindle CW, M05 = Spindle stop
- ☐ M06 = Tool change, M30 = Program end
- ☐ G90 = Absolute, G91 = Incremental

**Construction Features:**
- ☐ Polymer concrete bed > Cast iron (damping)
- ☐ Rolling guideways > Sliding (speed)
- ☐ HSK tool holders > BT (balance, stiffness)
- ☐ Carousel ATC for ≤24 tools, chain for >40

**Common KTU Errors to Avoid:**
- ❌ Confusing milling +Z (up) with turning +Z (toward tailstock)
- ❌ Forgetting to specify metric/imperial (G21/G20)
- ❌ Mixing G90 and G91 in same program
- ❌ Missing spindle stop before tool change
- ❌ Wrong coolant command (M08 vs M09)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** For any 14-mark question on CNC, ALWAYS include: (1) A labeled block diagram, (2) Working principle in step-by-step form, (3) At least one numerical example, (4) A comparison table where applicable. Examiners reward systematic, well-illustrated answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of CNC, Features of CNC Systems, Introduction to Part Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT415 - COMPUTER AIDING DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Functions of CNC, Features of CNC Systems, Introduction to Part Programming # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is CNC? — Formal Definition **Computer Numerical Control (CNC)** is a manufacturing methodology in which pre-programmed software and coded numerical instructions (typically G-codes and M-codes) direct the motion and operating parameters of machine tools. The control system decodes these instructions to drive servo/Stepper motors that precisely position the cutting tool (or workpiece) along multiple axes, enabling automated production of complex parts with high repeatability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** > CNC refers to the control of machine tool axes and auxiliary functions through numerical data processed by a dedicated microcomputer-based controller (typically a Fanuc, Siemens, or Heidenhain MCU), replacing traditional manual or cam-based automation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The CNC as a Robotic Chef" Imagine a highly skilled chef preparing a recipe: | Human Chef (Manual Machine) | CNC Machine (Automated) | |----------------------------|--------------------------| | Reads recipe from a book | Reads program from memory/USB | | Uses hands to mix, chop, cook | Uses servo motors to move tool | | Recalls recipe from memory | Stores program digitally | | Repeats inconsistently | Repeats with $\pm 0.001$ mm accuracy | > [!NOTE] > **The Core Idea:** A CNC machine is essentially a "robotic craftsman" that never tires, never deviates, and never forgets its instructions. The *program* is the recipe, the *controller* is the brain, and the *servo motors* are the muscles. ## 1.3 Functions of CNC — The Three Primary Motion Categories The KTU syllabus categorizes CNC functions based on the **type of motion path** the controller can produce. These are the three classical classifications: ### A. Point-to-Point (PTP) Control - The tool moves to a **discrete location**, performs an operation (like drilling, punching, tapping), then retracts and moves to the **next point**. - **Path between points is NOT controlled** — only the final position matters. - **Typical applications:** Drilling, boring, tapping, spot welding, punching. - **Machines:** Drill presses, boring machines, EDM (die-sinking). ### B. Straight-Line (Line) Control - Tool moves in **straight lines along a single axis** at controlled feed rate. - The controller can produce linear cuts, ramps, and chamfers — but **no curves**. - **Typical applications:** Turning straight diameters, milling rectangular pockets, face milling. - **Machines:** Light-duty lathes, simple milling machines. ### C. Contouring (Continuous Path) Control - Tool moves simultaneously along **two or more axes** to produce **continuous curved profiles** (2D and 3D). - Requires **interpolation** (linear and circular) — the controller calculates intermediate points between the start and end of a curve. - **Typical applications:** Mold/die making, turbine blades, complex 3D surfaces, aerospace components. - **Machines:** Machining centers, turning centers, 5-axis mills. ## 1.4 Auxiliary Functions of CNC (Beyond Cutting Motion) A CNC controller doesn't just move axes — it orchestrates an entire ecosystem of machine functions: 1. **Spindle Control** — Speed (RPM), direction (CW/CCW), stop. 2. **Tool Management** — Automatic tool change (ATC), tool length offset, tool diameter compensation, tool number selection. 3. **Coolant Control** — Flood coolant, mist coolant, M08/M09 codes. 4. **Feedrate Override** — Real-time adjustment of cutting speed via the operator panel. 5. **Work Coordinate System (WCS) Setting** — G54 to G59 for fixture offsets. 6. **Program Storage & Editing** — Internal memory, USB, DNC, Ethernet. 7. **Diagnostic & Alarms** — Self-diagnostics, error codes, axis overload alerts. 8. **Simulation & Dry Run** — Tool path verification before actual cutting. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** When asked "List the functions of CNC", KTU expects you to mention **all three motion functions (PTP, Straight-line, Contouring)** PLUS **at least four auxiliary functions** (spindle, tool change, coolant, feed override, etc.). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Comparison of CNC Motion Control Systems | Parameter | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Straight-Line | Contouring | |-----------|---------------------|---------------|------------| | Path control between points | ❌ Not controlled | ✅ Linear only | ✅ Linear + Circular + Spline | | Number of axes simultaneously controlled | 1 (sequential) | 1 (single axis) | 2 to 5+ (interpolated) | | Interpolation required | None | Not required for path | **Mandatory (linear & circular)** | | Positioning accuracy | High | High | Very high (with feedback) | | Typical machine | Drill press, EDM | Simple lathe | 3-axis VMC, 5-axis CNC | | Programming complexity | Low | Moderate | High | | Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest | | Example KTU application | PCB drilling | Facing, straight turning | Mold cavity machining | ## 2.2 Features of CNC Systems — The 12 Pillars The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically asks for **Features of CNC Systems**. These are the characteristic capabilities that distinguish CNC from conventional (manual) machines: ### 1. **Numerical Program Control** - Machine actions are governed by coded alphanumeric instructions. - Program resides in controller memory and is executed sequentially. ### 2. **High Flexibility & Quick Changeover** - Switching from one part to another requires only a **program change**, not a re-tooling or re-fixturing. - Ideal for **batch production** and **job shops**. ### 3. **High Precision & Repeatability** - Positioning accuracy: **± 0.005 mm** typical (precision class). - Repeatability: **± 0.002 mm** typical. - Achieved through **closed-loop feedback** (encoders, linear scales). ### 4. **Automatic Tool Change (ATC)** - Tool magazine (chain, drum, or rack type) holds 12 to 200+ tools. - Reduces non-cutting time dramatically. ### 5. **Multi-Axis Simultaneous Control** - 3-axis (X, Y, Z), 4-axis (adds A), 5-axis (adds B or rotary). - Enables manufacture of complex geometries in **one setup**. ### 6. **Feedback & Closed-Loop Control** - **Servo system** with positional feedback (encoder/resolver) continuously corrects axis position. - Eliminates accumulated error. ### 7. **Program Storage & Editing** - Programs stored in **CNC memory, USB, hard disk, or DNC networked server**. - Editable directly at the controller via MDI (Manual Data Input) or offline CAD/CAM. ### 8. **Auxiliary Function Control** - Spindle on/off, coolant on/off, chip conveyor, pallet changer, etc., all coordinated by program. ### 9. **Display & Diagnostics** - LCD/CRT display shows: program, current position, spindle load, alarm messages. - M-codes & G-codes shown in real-time. ### 10. **Dry Run & Single Block Execution** - **Dry Run:** Machine moves at rapid traverse with tool retracted to verify path. - **Single Block:** Executes one block at a time — used for program proving. ### 11. **Safety Interlocks** - Emergency stop, door interlock, overload alarms, limit switches, software stroke limits. ### 12. **Communication Interfaces** - RS-232, RS-422, Ethernet, USB, DNC (Direct Numerical Control), FMS integration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common mistake is to confuse *features* with *advantages*. **Features = built-in capabilities** (e.g., ATC, multi-axis control). **Advantages = benefits derived from features** (e.g., reduced cycle time, higher accuracy). Examiners allocate marks distinctly. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — CNC Motion & Programming | Concept | Formula / Standard Code | Units | Notes | |---------|------------------------|-------|-------| | Spindle speed | $N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | RPM | $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $D$ = diameter (mm) | | Feed rate (lathe) | $f_n = N \cdot f_r$ | mm/min | $f_r$ = feed per revolution (mm/rev) | | Feed rate (mill) | $f_n = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z \cdot n_{tooth}$ | mm/min | $Z$ = number of teeth, $n_{tooth}$ = flute count | | Block format | `N__ G__ X__ Y__ Z__ F__ S__ T__ M__` | — | Word-address format | | Linear interpolation | `G01 X__ Y__ Z__ F__` | — | Straight-line cut at feed F | | Circular CW/CCW | `G02 / G03 X__ Y__ R__ F__` | — | R = radius (or I, J, K incremental) | | Rapid traverse | `G00 X__ Y__ Z__` | — | Non-cutting positioning at max rate | | Absolute / Incremental | `G90` / `G91` | — | G90 uses workpiece origin; G91 uses current point | | Tool length offset | `G43 H__` | — | Compensates for tool length variance | | Cutter compensation | `G41 / G42 D__` | — | Left / Right of programmed path | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The above table uses `\vert` substitutes where absolute-value-style notation might be needed. **Never use a raw `|` character inside a markdown table cell** — it breaks the table parser in most KTU PDF renderers. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of CNC Systems | Industry Sector | CNC Application | |----------------|-----------------| | **Aerospace** | Turbine blades, structural ribs (5-axis contouring) | | **Automotive** | Engine blocks, cylinder heads, transmission housings | | **Medical** | Orthopedic implants, surgical instruments | | **Die & Mold** | Injection mold cavities, forging dies | | **Electronics** | PCB drilling, semiconductor wafer dicing | | **Defense** | Missile fin machining, gun barrel rifling | | **Energy** | Wind turbine hub, nuclear reactor components | ## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Contouring — Interpolation Explained **Why is contouring the most powerful CNC function?** Because the controller must compute **intermediate points** between the start and end of a curve — this is called **interpolation**. The two fundamental types are: 1. **Linear Interpolation (G01):** The controller calculates points along a straight line between two programmed endpoints. 2. **Circular Interpolation (G02/G03):** The controller calculates points along an arc, given the start point, end point, radius (or center), and direction (CW/CCW). > For complex 3D curves (sculpted surfaces), modern controllers add **spline interpolation** (NURBS) and **helical interpolation** — but these are beyond Module 3 scope. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Worked Examples ## 3.1 Anatomy of a CNC Part Program — Word-Address Format A CNC program is a **sequence of blocks (lines)**, each containing **words** (addresses with numeric values). ### Block Structure ``` N010 G01 X50.000 Y25.000 Z-5.000 F150 S1200 T01 M08 ; ``` Breaking it down word by word: | Word | Field | Value | Meaning | |------|-------|-------|---------| | N010 | Sequence number | 10 | Block identifier (for search & branching) | | G01 | Preparatory function | 01 | Linear interpolation (controlled feed) | | X, Y, Z | Axis position | 50, 25, -5 | Target coordinates in active WCS (mm) | | F150 | Feedrate | 150 mm/min | Cutting feed rate | | S1200 | Spindle speed | 1200 RPM | Spindle rotational speed | | T01 | Tool selection | Tool #1 | Calls tool from magazine | | M08 | Miscellaneous function | 08 | Coolant ON (flood) | ## 3.2 Worked Example: Calculating Spindle Speed **Problem:** A mild steel workpiece of diameter 80 mm is to be turned on a CNC lathe. The recommended cutting speed is 120 m/min. Calculate the required spindle speed. **Step 1 — Identify the given data:** - Diameter of workpiece, $D = 80 \text{ mm}$ - Cutting speed, $V = 120 \text{ m/min}$ **Step 2 — Apply the spindle speed formula:** $$ N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values:** $$ N = \frac{1000 \cdot 120}{\pi \cdot 80} $$ **Step 4 — Simplify the denominator:** $$ N = \frac{120000}{251.327} $$ **Step 5 — Final result:** $$ N = 477.46 \text{ RPM} $$ **Step 6 — Round to controller-compatible value (S-Code):** $$ S478 \text{ (or S480, depending on controller granularity)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The factor **1000** converts meters to millimeters because $D$ is in mm but $V$ is in m/min. This is a frequent dimensional error in KTU exams. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Feed Rate Calculation on a CNC Mill **Problem:** A 4-flute end mill (Ø 12 mm) runs at 3000 RPM with a feed per tooth of 0.05 mm. Calculate the feed rate (mm/min). **Step 1 — Identify variables:** - Number of teeth, $Z = 4$ - Spindle speed, $N = 3000 \text{ RPM}$ - Feed per tooth, $f_z = 0.05 \text{ mm/tooth}$ **Step 2 — Apply the feed rate formula:** $$ f_n = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z $$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ f_n = 3000 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 4 $$ **Step 4 — Compute:** $$ f_n = 600 \text{ mm/min} $$ **Step 5 — The F-word in the program becomes:** ``` F600 ``` ## 3.4 Worked Example: Manual Part Program for a Simple Stepped Shaft **Component:** A stepped shaft (turned on a CNC lathe) with: - Face turning from X52 to X0 - Turn Ø30 mm for 20 mm length - Turn Ø20 mm for 15 mm length - Cutoff at X0, Z-40 **Program (Fanuc-style, work zero at face center):** | Block | Code | Explanation | |-------|------|-------------| | N05 | `G21 G90 G40` | Metric, absolute mode, cutter comp OFF | | N10 | `M08` | Coolant ON | | N15 | `G00 X52.0 Z2.0 T01` | Rapid to start point, Tool 1 | | N20 | `G97 S2000` | Constant spindle speed 2000 RPM | | N25 | `G00 X52.0 Z0` | Rapid to face start | | N30 | `G01 X0 F0.2` | Face turn at 0.2 mm/rev | | N35 | `G00 X30.0 Z2.0` | Rapid to OD30 start | | N40 | `G01 Z-20.0 F0.15` | Turn Ø30 over 20 mm | | N45 | `G00 X20.0 Z2.0` | Rapid to OD20 start | | N50 | `G01 Z-15.0 F0.15` | Turn Ø20 over 15 mm | | N55 | `G00 X55.0 Z5.0` | Retract tool | | N60 | `M09` | Coolant OFF | | N65 | `M05` | Spindle stop | | N70 | `M30` | Program end, rewind | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** In manual part programming, always **state the coordinate system assumption** (e.g., "Work zero at the face, +Z points away from chuck"). Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous origin declarations. ## 3.5 Comparison Table: Manual vs. CAM-Based Part Programming | Parameter | Manual Part Programming | CAM-Based Programming | |-----------|------------------------|----------------------| | Method | Direct G-code writing by programmer | Software (Mastercam, Fusion 360, NX) generates code | | Skill required | Deep G/M-code knowledge | CAD modeling + CAM software operation | | Best for | Simple 2D/2.5D parts, lathe operations | Complex 3D contours, 5-axis surfaces | | Time for complex part | Days/weeks | Hours | | Error rate | High (typo in code = crash) | Low (post-processor validated) | | KTU syllabus weight | **High (Module 3 focus)** | Mentioned only briefly | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CNC Machine — Functional Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Part Program Storage
USB, DNC, Memory] --> B[Machine Control Unit MCU
Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain] B --> C[Interpreter and Decoder
Block by block execution] C --> D[Interpolator
Linear, Circular, Helical] D --> E[Servo Drive Amplifiers
X, Y, Z, A, B axes] E --> F[Servo Motors and Ball Screws
Physical axis motion] F --> G[Position Feedback
Encoders and Linear Scales] G --> D B --> H[Spindle Drive and Motor] B --> I[Auxiliary Logic
Coolant, ATC, Chip Conveyor] H --> J[Spindle and Tool] I --> J J --> K[Workpiece and Machined Part] B --> L[Operator Panel and HMI
Display, Override, Edit] L --> B B --> M[Diagnostics and Alarms] M --> B ``` ## 4.2 CNC Part Programming Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1 Engineering Drawing Analysis] --> P2[Step 2 Process Planning
Operations, Tools, Speeds] P2 --> P3[Step 3 Work Zero and Fixture Setup] P3 --> P4[Step 4 Write Program
Word Address Format] P4 --> P5[Step 5 Syntax Verification
Offline or Editor] P5 --> P6{Step 6 Dry Run
Tool Retracted} P6 -->|Path OK| P7[Step 7 Single Block
Proving Cuts] P6 -->|Path Error| P4 P7 -->|Verified| P8[Step 8 Full Production Run] P7 -->|Alarm or Crash| P4 P8 --> P9[Step 9 First Article Inspection] P9 -->|Within Tolerance| P10[Step 10 Batch Production] P9 -->|Out of Tolerance| P2 ``` ## 4.3 Classification of CNC Motion Control Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB Root[CNC Machine Tools] --> PTP[Point to Point Control] Root --> SL[Straight Line Control] Root --> CC[Contouring Control] PTP --> PTPex[Examples Drill Press
EDM, Boring, Tapping] SL --> SLex[Examples Simple Lathe
Linear Milling] CC --> CC2D[2 Axis Contouring
G01 and G02 G03] CC --> CC3D[3 Axis Contouring
3D Surfaces] CC --> CC5A[5 Axis Simultaneous
Aerospace Molds] CC2D --> Int1[Requires Linear and
Circular Interpolation] CC3D --> Int1 CC5A --> Int1 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo System (Why CNC is Accurate) ```mermaid flowchart LR Cmd[Command Pulse from MCU] --> Comp[Comparator] Fb[Feedback from Encoder] --> Comp Comp -->|Error Signal| Amp[Servo Amplifier] Amp --> Motor[Servo Motor] Motor --> BS[Ball Screw] BS --> Tbl[Machine Slide or Spindle] Tbl --> EncoderRot[Rotary Encoder on Motor] EncoderRot --> Fb Tbl --> LinearScale[Optional Linear Scale
Direct Position Read] LinearScale --> Fb ``` > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The **comparator** continuously subtracts *actual position* (from encoder) from *commanded position* (from MCU). Any difference becomes an *error signal* that drives the motor until the error is zero. This is the physical basis of CNC's **± 0.005 mm accuracy**. ## 4.5 CNC Part Program Structure — Hierarchical View ```mermaid flowchart TD Prog[Complete CNC Part Program] --> Hdr[Header Section
G21, G90, G40, M08, S, T] Hdr --> Body[Body Section
Main cutting blocks G00 to G01 G02 G03] Body --> Tail[Tail Section
M09, M05, G00 to safe, M30] Body --> Op1[Operation 1 Facing] Body --> Op2[Operation 2 Rough Turning] Body --> Op3[Operation 3 Finish Turning] Body --> Op4[Operation 4 Cutoff or Threading] Tail --> End[End of Program Marker] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three functions of a CNC machine tool.** **Model Answer:** The three primary functions of a CNC machine tool are: 1. **Point-to-Point (PTP) Control** — Positioning the tool at discrete locations for operations like drilling and tapping, where the path between points is uncontrolled. 2. **Straight-Line Control** — Moving the tool along a single linear axis at a controlled feed rate for straight cuts, facings, and simple rectangular profiles. 3. **Contouring (Continuous Path) Control** — Moving the tool simultaneously along two or more axes using **linear and circular interpolation** to produce curved 2D and 3D profiles. *Auxiliary functions include spindle control, automatic tool change, coolant control, and feedrate override.* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between point-to-point and contouring CNC systems (any four points).** **Model Answer:** | Sl. | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Contouring (Continuous Path) | |-----|----------------------|------------------------------| | 1 | Only the **end position** is controlled | The **entire path** between start and end is controlled | | 2 | **No interpolation** is required | **Linear and circular interpolation** is mandatory | | 3 | Only **one axis moves at a time** | **Two or more axes move simultaneously** | | 4 | Used for **drilling, boring, tapping, EDM** | Used for **milling curves, mold cavities, turbine blades** | | 5 | **Simple, low-cost** controller | **Complex, expensive** controller with high-end MCU | --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **functions of CNC** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **features of a CNC system**. List any **six features**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** The functions of CNC are classified into **three motion control categories** plus several **auxiliary functions**. **1. Point-to-Point (PTP) Control:** - Tool travels to a defined coordinate, performs an operation, then withdraws and moves to the next point. - The controller does **not control the path** traversed between points — only the **final position** is accurate. - **Examples:** Drilling, reaming, tapping, boring, spot welding, die-sinking EDM. - **Typical machine:** Radial drill press, CNC EDM. **2. Straight-Line Control:** - Tool moves along **a single axis** at a programmed feed rate in a straight line. - Suitable for operations requiring straight cuts without curvature. - **Examples:** Turning cylindrical diameters, facing, slotting, face milling. - **Typical machine:** CNC lathe (in 2-axis mode), basic milling machine. **3. Contouring (Continuous Path) Control:** - The controller drives **two or more axes simultaneously** to generate **continuous 2D and 3D profiles**. - Requires **interpolation**: - **Linear interpolation (G01)** for straight-line cuts. - **Circular interpolation (G02 / G03)** for arcs. - **Examples:** Machining of dies, molds, turbine blades, cams, aerospace structural parts. - **Typical machine:** 3-axis VMC, 5-axis machining center, CNC turning center with C-axis. **Auxiliary Functions (briefly):** spindle speed/direction control, tool magazine management, coolant on/off, pallet changer, chip conveyor, automatic door, lighting — all coordinated by M-codes. > **Diagrammatic Representation (Text Form):** > > - **PTP:** A → (any path) → B → (any path) → C (only endpoints matter) > - **Straight-line:** A → (straight line) → B > - **Contouring:** A → (smooth curve) → B (full path is precise) --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Block Diagram of a CNC System (Functional):** ``` [Part Program] → [MCU (Microcomputer)] → [Servo Drives] → [Servo Motors] → [Machine Tool] ↑ ↓ [Feedback Sensors] ←——— [Position/Speed Encoders] ↓ [Operator Panel + Display + Diagnostics] ``` **Six Key Features of a CNC System:** 1. **Program-Controlled Operation:** All machine actions are governed by a stored alphanumeric program (G-codes and M-codes), ensuring identical execution for every cycle. 2. **High Flexibility:** Changeover from one component to another requires only a new program — no major mechanical reconfiguration. Ideal for small batch sizes and high part variety. 3. **High Precision and Repeatability:** Positioning accuracy up to **± 0.005 mm** and repeatability up to **± 0.002 mm**, achieved through closed-loop servo feedback. 4. **Multi-Axis Simultaneous Interpolation:** Up to 5 axes can move together with linear, circular, and helical interpolation, enabling complex surface machining in a single setup. 5. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC):** A tool magazine (chain, drum, or carousel) holds 12 to 200+ tools; tool changes occur automatically based on T-word in the program. 6. **Built-in Diagnostics and Safety:** Real-time alarms, software stroke limits, door interlocks, and emergency stop circuits protect both the machine and the operator. *Other notable features:* Dry-run mode, single-block execution, DNC networking, in-process measurement, automatic pallet changers. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** **(a)** What is **part programming**? Explain the difference between **manual part programming** and **CAM-based part programming** with examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a turning operation on mild steel, the cutting speed is 180 m/min and the workpiece diameter is 60 mm. **Calculate the spindle speed**. If the feed is 0.2 mm/rev, calculate the **feed rate in mm/min**. Write a **sample CNC program** to face the workpiece from X65 to X0. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Part Programming:** Part programming is the process of preparing a sequence of coded instructions (G-codes, M-codes, coordinates, speeds, and feeds) that the CNC controller interprets to manufacture a specific component. **Types of Part Programming:** | Aspect | Manual Part Programming | CAM-Based Part Programming | |--------|------------------------|----------------------------| | Definition | Programmer writes each line of G-code by hand | Software (e.g., Mastercam, Fusion 360) generates G-code from a 3D CAD model | | Tools Used | Pencil, paper, controller's MDI | CAD model + CAM software + post-processor | | Skill Required | Deep knowledge of G-codes, M-codes, trigonometry, geometry | CAD modeling + CAM toolpath strategy | | Best Suited For | Simple 2D parts, lathe operations, drilling | Complex 3D contours, molds, aerospace parts | | Time Required | Long for complex parts | Significantly reduced | | Error Risk | High (manual typo causes crash) | Low (post-processor validated) | | Example | Writing a turning program for a stepped shaft | Generating a 5-axis toolpath for a turbine blade | **Example of manual programming (turning, 2-line extract):** ``` N30 G00 X65.0 Z2.0 N40 G01 X0 F0.2 ``` **Example of CAM-based workflow (text):** 1. Create 3D model in CAD. 2. Define stock, fixture, and work zero. 3. Select 2D Contour toolpath strategy. 4. Specify tool (Ø12 mm end mill), RPM, feed. 5. Post-process to generate Fanuc G-code. --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** - Cutting speed, $V = 180 \text{ m/min}$ - Workpiece diameter, $D = 60 \text{ mm}$ - Feed per revolution, $f_r = 0.2 \text{ mm/rev}$ **Step 1 — Spindle Speed Calculation:** $$ N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} $$ $$ N = \frac{1000 \cdot 180}{\pi \cdot 60} $$ $$ N = \frac{180000}{188.496} $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx 955 \text{ RPM}} $$ **Step 2 — Feed Rate Calculation (Lathe):** $$ f_n = N \cdot f_r $$ $$ f_n = 955 \cdot 0.2 $$ $$ \boxed{f_n = 191 \text{ mm/min}} $$ **Step 3 — Sample CNC Program for Facing (X65 → X0):** | Block | Code | Description | |-------|------|-------------| | N05 | `O0001` | Program number | | N10 | `G21 G90 G40` | Metric, absolute, cutter comp off | | N15 | `M08` | Coolant ON | | N20 | `T0101` | Tool 1, offset 1 (facing tool) | | N25 | `G97 S955` | Constant spindle speed 955 RPM | | N30 | `G00 X65.0 Z0.5` | Rapid to start point near face | | N35 | `G01 X0 F191` | Face turn at 191 mm/min to center | | N40 | `G00 X65.0 Z2.0` | Retract | | N45 | `M09` | Coolant OFF | | N50 | `M05` | Spindle stop | | N55 | `M30` | Program end, rewind | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Functions vs. Features Confusion** — Functions describe *what the machine does* (PTP, contouring, spindle control). Features describe *built-in capabilities* (ATC, multi-axis, diagnostics). Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for interchanging them. > > 2. **Wrong Unit in Spindle Speed Formula** — The 1000 conversion factor is **frequently forgotten**, leading to an answer of 1.5 RPM instead of 1500 RPM. Always show the **unit cancellation** explicitly. > > 3. **Missing Work-Zero Declaration in Programs** — A CNC program without a clear coordinate system assumption (G54, G90, etc.) is treated as **incomplete** by the KTU examiner. > > 4. **Confusing G90 (Absolute) with G91 (Incremental)** — Writing a G-code program in incremental mode when it should be absolute (or vice versa) is a **silent crash risk** in real machines, and a **2-mark deduction** in exams. > > 5. **Mixing Lathe and Mill Terminology** — Feed per *revolution* (mm/rev) is for turning; feed per *tooth* (mm/tooth) is for milling. Wrong formula application loses full marks. > > 6. **Forgetting M30 / Program End** — A program without `M30` is considered **non-executable** by the controller and will not rewind. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Three motion functions of CNC:** Point-to-Point, Straight-Line, Contouring — listed in increasing order of complexity. - ✅ **Contouring requires interpolation** — linear (G01) and circular (G02/G03) are the minimum; spline/helical are advanced. - ✅ **PTP is for drilling/boring**; **Straight-line is for simple facing/turning**; **Contouring is for curves and 3D surfaces**. - ✅ **Six flagship features of CNC:** Program control, flexibility, high accuracy, multi-axis interpolation, ATC, diagnostics/safety. - ✅ **Spindle speed formula:** $\displaystyle N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ (always in RPM, $V$ in m/min, $D$ in mm). - ✅ **Lathe feed formula:** $\displaystyle f_n = N \cdot f_r$ (mm/min). - ✅ **Mill feed formula:** $\displaystyle f_n = N \cdot f_z \cdot Z$ (mm/min). - ✅ **Part program block format:** `N__ G__ X__ Y__ Z__ F__ S__ T__ M__` (word-address). - ✅ **Manual programming** = hand-written G-code; **CAM programming** = software-generated G-code. - ✅ **Always end a program with M30** to rewind and signal completion. - ✅ **G90 = absolute**, **G91 = incremental**, **G21 = metric**, **G20 = inch** — these are the "house rules" of any CNC program. - ✅ **KTU exam focus:** definition + classification (functions), 6–8 features with examples, one small manual program with calculation — together these cover 80% of past paper marks.

topics.title: Simple Turning and Drilling Programs Using G Codes and M Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT415 - COMPUTER AIDED DESIGN AND MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simple Turning and Drilling Programs Using G Codes and M Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machining, **G-Codes** and **M-Codes** form the standardized instruction set (ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267) used to command machine tool motions and auxiliary functions. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for CAD/CAM, a CNC part program is a structured sequence of these alphanumeric addresses that directs the controller to perform machining operations such as **Simple Turning** (on a CNC Lathe) and **Drilling** (using the tailstock or a cross-slide mounted drill chuck on a lathe).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> **G-Codes (Preparatory Functions):** Modal/non-modal codes that define the *type of motion* or *interpolation mode* (e.g., linear, circular, rapid) and the *coordinate system reference* before tool movement occurs.
> **M-Codes (Miscellaneous Functions):** Codes that control the *auxiliary on/off* functions of the machine, such as spindle rotation, coolant flow, tool changes, and program termination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are giving driving directions to a friend who is driving a truck. Before the trip, you would need to tell them:
1. **The Mode of Travel** (highway, city streets, off-road) — This is exactly what a **G-Code** does. It sets the *type of motion* (rapid traverse, linear feed, circular arc).
2. **The Vehicle Controls** (turn on engine, turn on headlights, turn on wipers, refuel) — This is what an **M-Code** does. It controls the *auxiliary switches* of the machine (spindle on, coolant on, program stop).

In a CNC lathe:
- **Turning** is the "city driving" — the tool moves along defined paths (G01) to cut material.
- **Drilling** is the "off-road detour" — the tool plunges straight in along a single axis to make a hole.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in CNC Turning

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference Values:**
> - **Spindle Speed (N):** Revolutions per minute (**RPM**)
> - **Feed Rate (F):** mm/rev for turning (typical range: **0.05 – 0.5 mm/rev**)
> - **Cutting Speed (Vc):** m/min (typical mild steel turning: **120 – 250 m/min**)
> - **Coordinate System:** Right-hand rule, **+X = away from spindle axis (diameter direction)**, **+Z = away from chuck (towards tailstock)**
> - **Units:** Default metric (**G21**) — millimeters

### 1.4 The Two Standard Coordinate Systems for CNC Lathe

| Axis | Direction | Typical Reference |
|------|-----------|-------------------|
| **X** | Radial (diameter) | Tool moves away from centerline for +X |
| **Z** | Longitudinal | Tool moves towards tailstock for +Z |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike milling where X and Y are linear distances, on a CNC **Lathe**, the **X-axis always represents the DIAMETER** of the workpiece. A movement of "X10" means moving to a diameter of **10 mm**, not a 10 mm radius.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of G-Codes (Preparatory Functions)

G-codes are categorized based on their function. Below is the high-yield classification structured for KTU examination preparation:

**A. Motion Commands (Most frequently asked in KTU):**
- **G00** — Rapid Traverse (no cutting, fastest positioning)
- **G01** — Linear Interpolation (straight-line cutting at programmed feed)
- **G02** — Circular Interpolation Clockwise (CW)
- **G03** — Circular Interpolation Counter-Clockwise (CCW)

**B. Coordinate System & Units:**
- **G90** — Absolute Programming (coordinates referenced from part zero)
- **G91** — Incremental Programming (coordinates referenced from current position)
- **G20** — Inch input mode
- **G21** — Millimeter input mode

**C. Spindle & Feed Control (CNC Lathe specific):**
- **G94** — Feed per minute (mm/min)
- **G95** — Feed per revolution (mm/rev) — **Default for turning**
- **G96** — Constant Surface Speed (CSS) control
- **G97** — Constant Spindle Speed (RPM) control

**D. Canned Cycles (Used for Drilling on Lathe):**
- **G81** — Simple Drilling Cycle (Drill, Retract)
- **G83** — Peck Drilling Cycle (Deep hole drilling with chip breaking)

### 2.2 Classification of M-Codes (Miscellaneous Functions)

| M-Code | Function | Typical Use Case |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| **M00** | Program Stop | Mandatory halt, operator intervention |
| **M01** | Optional Stop | Conditional halt (with operator switch ON) |
| **M03** | Spindle ON (CW) | Main cutting direction |
| **M04** | Spindle ON (CCW) | Back-turning or special tools |
| **M05** | Spindle OFF | End of operation |
| **M06** | Tool Change | ATC (Automatic Tool Change) |
| **M08** | Coolant ON | Flood coolant for turning |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF | Stop coolant flow |
| **M30** | Program End & Rewind | End of part program |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | **rev/min (RPM)** | Turning speed calculation |
| **Cutting Speed (Vc)** | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | **m/min** | Given N and D |
| **Feed Rate (F)** | $F = f \cdot N$ | **mm/min** | When using G94 mode |
| **Feed per Rev (f)** | $f = \dfrac{F}{N}$ | **mm/rev** | G95 mode (default) |
| **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V_c$ | **mm³/min** | For turning |
| **Drill Time (T)** | $T = \dfrac{L + L_a}{f \cdot N}$ | **minutes** | For drilling cycle |
| **Drill Approach (La)** | $L_a = 0.3 \cdot D$ | **mm** | Standard drill point allowance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Note:** In formula sheets and derivations, you will **frequently be asked to calculate the Spindle Speed (N)** before writing the CNC program. Always show the substitution step explicitly.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CNC turning and drilling programs using G-codes and M-codes are the backbone of:
- **Aerospace industry:** Turning of turbine shafts with deep hole drilling for cooling passages.
- **Automotive industry:** Mass production of crankshafts, axles, and cylindrical engine components.
- **Medical implants:** Precision turning of bone screws with subsequent drilling for surgical pins.
- **Hydraulic/Pneumatic fittings:** Cylindrical bodies with cross-drilled ports.

The **G81 drilling cycle** is especially critical in production environments because it reduces programming time, ensures consistent hole depth, and prevents drill breakage in deep holes (improved by **G83** peck drilling).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Programs

### 3.1 The Logical Framework of a CNC Part Program

Every CNC part program follows this structured sequence. Understanding this framework is essential for KTU exam problems:

**Step 1: Initialization Block**
- Set units (G21 for mm), absolute mode (G90), feed mode (G95).
- Set tool, spindle speed, spindle ON (M03), coolant ON (M08).

**Step 2: Tool Positioning**
- Rapid move (G00) to a safe clearance point near the workpiece.

**Step 3: Machining Operations**
- Linear cuts (G01) for turning diameters.
- Canned cycle (G81) for drilling.

**Step 4: Tool Retract & End**
- Rapid retract, spindle OFF (M05), coolant OFF (M09), program end (M30).

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Simple Turning Program (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** 
> Write a CNC turning program to reduce a workpiece from **Ø50 mm to Ø30 mm** over a length of **40 mm**. The starting point is at **Z2.0** (2 mm away from the chuck face). Use:
> - Tool: T01 (Turning tool, RH)
> - Spindle Speed: **1500 RPM**
> - Feed: **0.2 mm/rev**
> - Coolant: ON
> - Depth of cut: **2 mm per pass** (finish in 2 passes for Ø30)

**Step-by-Step Program Derivation:**

**Line 1:** Program Number Identification
```
O1001 ;
```
*Logic:* Standard FANUC convention. The letter 'O' followed by a 4-digit number identifies the program.

**Line 2:** Initialization & Units
```
G21 G90 G95 G97 ;
```
*Logic:* 
- G21 → Millimeter input
- G90 → Absolute coordinate mode (coordinates measured from part zero)
- G95 → Feed per revolution (correct for turning)
- G97 → Constant RPM mode

**Line 3:** Tool Selection
```
T0101 ;
```
*Logic:* First two digits '01' = tool number; second two digits '01' = tool offset register.

**Line 4:** Spindle Start
```
M03 S1500 ;
```
*Logic:* M03 → Spindle ON clockwise; S1500 → 1500 RPM (per problem data).

**Line 5:** Coolant ON
```
M08 ;
```
*Logic:* Flood coolant for turning.

**Line 6:** Rapid to Safe Approach Point
```
G00 X52.0 Z2.0 ;
```
*Logic:* G00 rapid move. X52.0 = approach 2 mm beyond Ø50 (clearance). Z2.0 = 2 mm from part face.

**Line 7:** First Turning Pass (Linear Interpolation)
```
G01 X46.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
```
*Logic:* 
- G01 → Linear feed (cutting motion)
- X46.0 → Reduce from Ø50 to Ø46 (depth of cut = 2 mm radially)
- Z-40.0 → Cut along 40 mm length
- F0.2 → Feed at 0.2 mm/rev

**Step-by-Step Mathematical Verification:**

The depth of cut per pass is:
$$d = \frac{D_{initial} - D_{final}}{2 \times N_{passes}}$$

$$d = \frac{50 - 30}{2 \times 2} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ mm radial depth}$$

Wait — re-checking the problem: depth of cut is given as 2 mm per pass. So for Ø30 final, with 2 mm per pass, we need (50-30)/2 = 10 mm total radial removal = 5 passes.

**Correction — Revised Program (5 passes of 2 mm radial each):**

**Line 7 (Pass 1):**
```
G01 X46.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
```
*Logic:* Ø50 → Ø46 (2 mm radial cut).

**Line 7b (Retract to safe X):**
```
G00 X52.0 ;
```
*Logic:* Rapid retract in X.

**Line 7c (Return to start Z):**
```
G00 Z2.0 ;
```
*Logic:* Return to start point in Z.

**Line 8 (Pass 2):**
```
G01 X42.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
```
*Logic:* Cut to Ø42 (4 mm radial total).

**Line 9 (Pass 3):**
```
G01 X38.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
```
*Logic:* Cut to Ø38 (6 mm radial total).

**Line 10 (Pass 4):**
```
G01 X34.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
```
*Logic:* Cut to Ø34 (8 mm radial total).

**Line 11 (Pass 5 - Final):**
```
G01 X30.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
```
*Logic:* Final pass to Ø30 (10 mm radial total).

**Line 12:** Rapid Retract
```
G00 X100.0 Z100.0 ;
```
*Logic:* Move to a safe tool change/home position (typical: X100 Z100).

**Line 13:** Spindle & Coolant OFF
```
M05 ;
M09 ;
```
*Logic:* Stop spindle and coolant before program termination.

**Line 14:** Program End
```
M30 ;
```
*Logic:* End of program and rewind to beginning.

**Complete Final Program:**
```
O1001 ;
G21 G90 G95 G97 ;
T0101 ;
M03 S1500 ;
M08 ;
G00 X52.0 Z2.0 ;
G01 X46.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
G00 X52.0 ;
G00 Z2.0 ;
G01 X42.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
G00 X52.0 ;
G00 Z2.0 ;
G01 X38.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
G00 X52.0 ;
G00 Z2.0 ;
G01 X34.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
G00 X52.0 ;
G00 Z2.0 ;
G01 X30.0 Z-40.0 F0.2 ;
G00 X100.0 Z100.0 ;
M05 ;
M09 ;
M30 ;
```

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Simple Drilling Program (Step-by-Step with G81)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> A workpiece of **Ø40 mm** diameter requires a **center hole** at the face (along Z-axis) to a depth of **15 mm** using a **Ø8 mm drill**. The drill is held in the tailstock. Write the CNC lathe program. Use:
> - Drill: T05
> - Spindle Speed: **800 RPM**
> - Feed: **0.1 mm/rev**
> - Coolant: ON

**Step-by-Step Program Derivation:**

**Step 1: Calculate Drill Approach Distance (La):**
$$L_a = 0.3 \times D = 0.3 \times 8 = 2.4 \text{ mm}$$

This is the additional drill travel beyond the hole depth to clear the drill point.

**Step 2: Calculate R-point (Reference Plane) Position:**
The drill starts at Z2.0 (clearance from face). R-point = Z2.0 (where drill begins cutting feed).

**Step 3: Calculate Z-point (Bottom of Hole):**
$$Z = -(L) = -15.0 \text{ mm}$$ 
This is the actual bottom of the hole. The drill will feed to this point.

**Step 4: Program Writing with G81 Canned Cycle:**

**Line 1:** Program Number
```
O2002 ;
```

**Line 2:** Safe Initialization
```
G21 G90 G95 G97 ;
```

**Line 3:** Tool Selection (Drill)
```
T0505 ;
```
*Logic:* Tool 5, offset register 5.

**Line 4:** Spindle ON
```
M03 S800 ;
```

**Line 5:** Coolant ON
```
M08 ;
```

**Line 6:** Rapid to Drill Start Position
```
G00 X0 Z2.0 ;
```
*Logic:* X0 = Centerline of workpiece. Z2.0 = 2 mm clearance from face.

**Step 7: Drilling Canned Cycle (G81)**

The G81 format on a CNC lathe is:
```
G81 Z__ R__ F__ ;
```
Where:
- **Z** = Final depth of hole (Z-point)
- **R** = R-plane (rapid plane) - position above workpiece where drill transitions from rapid to feed
- **F** = Feed rate

**Drilling Block:**
```
G81 Z-15.0 R2.0 F0.1 ;
```
*Logic:*
- G81 → Simple drilling canned cycle
